name: Cloudflare Access Service Token
description: Creates a machine-to-machine Service Token for Cloudflare Access. This generates a Client ID and Client Secret pair used to authenticate automated services (e.g. cron jobs, agents, CI/CD) against Access-protected applications without user interaction.
prereqs: A Cloudflare account with a Zero Trust (Access) plan enabled.
At least one Access application already configured that the service token will authenticate against.
Permission to create service tokens in the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard (Super Administrator or Access administrator role).

steps.title: Open the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard
steps.instruction: Navigate to the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard. If you have multiple accounts, select the account that owns the Access application you want to authenticate against.

steps.title: Navigate to Service Auth
steps.instruction: In the left sidebar, expand 'Access' and click 'Service Auth'. This page lists all existing service tokens for the account.
steps.screenshot_hint: Left sidebar showing Access > Service Auth menu item.

steps.title: Create a new Service Token
steps.instruction: Click the 'Create Service Token' button at the top of the page.
steps.screenshot_hint: Blue 'Create Service Token' button at the top right of the Service Auth page.

steps.title: Name the Service Token
steps.instruction: Enter a descriptive name for the token. Use something that identifies the machine or service that will use it, e.g. 'rialu-agent-prod' or 'ci-deploy-bot'. Choose a token duration or leave as non-expiring if appropriate.

steps.title: Generate the token
steps.instruction: Click 'Generate token' (or 'Create Service Token'). Cloudflare will display both the Client ID and the Client Secret on the next screen.
steps.warning: The Client Secret is shown only once. If you close this page without copying it, you will need to rotate the token to get a new secret.

steps.title: Copy the Client ID
steps.instruction: Copy the 'CF-Access-Client-Id' value displayed on screen. This is a short UUID-style identifier. It is not secret but is required alongside the secret for authentication.
steps.screenshot_hint: A text field labelled 'CF-Access-Client-Id' with a copy button.

steps.title: Copy the Client Secret
steps.instruction: Copy the 'CF-Access-Client-Secret' value displayed on screen. This is a long random string. Store it securely.
steps.screenshot_hint: A text field labelled 'CF-Access-Client-Secret' with a copy button.

steps.title: Add a Service Auth policy to your Access application
steps.instruction: Go to Access > Applications, select the application you want this token to reach, and edit its policies. Add (or confirm) a policy with Action 'Service Auth' that includes this service token. Without this policy the token will be rejected even though it is valid.
steps.warning: The service token alone does not grant access. You must also add a Service Auth policy rule on the target Access application.

steps.title: Test the token
steps.instruction:
Verify the token works by making a request to your Access-protected endpoint with both headers set:

curl -H 'CF-Access-Client-Id: <your-client-id>' -H 'CF-Access-Client-Secret: <your-client-secret>' https://your-app.example.com/

A successful response (HTTP 200) means the token and policy are configured correctly. A 403 means the policy is missing or misconfigured.

vault_entry.notes: Store both CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID and CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET. The client ID is not secret but is required for authentication. Both values are shown only once at creation time.
